heroOverview: Homocystinuria (ICD-10: E72.11) is a group of inherited metabolic disorders in which the sulfur-containing amino acid homocysteine accumulates in the blood and urine because of defects in the enzymes or cofactors that process it. The most common cause — deficiency of cystathionine beta-synthase (CBS) — affects approximately 1 in 200,000 to 335,000 births worldwide, though some populations such as Ireland and Qatar have considerably higher rates. Untreated, homocysteine damages blood vessels and connective tissue, producing a striking clinical triad of tall stature with skeletal deformities resembling Marfan syndrome, dislocated eye lenses, intellectual disability, and early, life-threatening thromboembolism. When detected through newborn screening and treated promptly with pyridoxine, dietary restriction, and betaine, the majority of patients avoid the most severe complications and lead relatively normal lives.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 200,000–335,000 births globally for CBS deficiency; up to 1 in 65,000 in Ireland (NCBI GeneReviews, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Autosomal recessive; equal sex distribution; presents in infancy or early childhood if not screened at birth
keyStats.avgAge: Symptoms first detectable in early childhood; major complications such as lens dislocation emerge by age 5–10 in untreated patients
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 3,000–5,000 diagnosed patients in Western countries; likely under-diagnosed globally

definition: Homocystinuria (ICD-10: E72.11) encompasses several inherited defects that cause homocysteine to accumulate in the blood (hyperhomocysteinemia) and spill into the urine. The most common form, classical homocystinuria, results from autosomal recessive deficiency of cystathionine beta-synthase (CBS), the enzyme that condenses homocysteine with serine to form cystathionine in the transsulfuration pathway. Without functional CBS, homocysteine — normally a transient intermediate in methionine catabolism — accumulates, is partially remethylated back to methionine (producing hypermethioninemia), and damages endothelial cells, elastic fibres, and platelets through multiple mechanisms including oxidative stress, impaired collagen cross-linking, and endothelial dysfunction. Less common causes include defects in methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR) and in cobalamin (vitamin B12) metabolism — including cobalamin C, D, E, F, G, and J deficiencies — which impair the remethylation of homocysteine to methionine; these forms are associated with low or normal methionine. The disorder sits within metabolic medicine and endocrinology; vascular medicine, ophthalmology, and clinical genetics are frequently co-managing disciplines. Classical CBS deficiency causes intellectual disability in a majority of untreated patients and a cumulative thromboembolism rate of approximately 25% by age 15 and over 50% by age 30.

typesClassification.description: Roughly 50% of CBS-deficient patients respond to high-dose pyridoxine (vitamin B6), which enhances residual CBS enzyme activity; this form tends to have a milder biochemical phenotype and better long-term outcomes.
The remainder require dietary methionine restriction plus betaine therapy to lower homocysteine; vascular risk is higher without strict metabolic control.
Severe MTHFR deficiency causes homocystinuria with low methionine, neurological deterioration, and a distinct presentation requiring folate and betaine rather than pyridoxine or diet restriction.

typesClassification.type: Cobalamin metabolism defects (cblC, cblE, cblG, etc.)
typesClassification.description: Various intracellular cobalamin processing defects impair methionine synthase and cause combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria; treated with hydroxocobalamin injections.

primarySymptoms: Ectopia lentis — downward and inward dislocation of the eye lens — causing progressive myopia, secondary glaucoma, and, if untreated, retinal detachment and blindness.
Marfanoid skeletal habitus: tall stature, long limbs, arachnodactyly (spider-like fingers), scoliosis, pectus deformity (carinatum or excavatum), and genu valgum.
Intellectual disability or cognitive impairment, present in approximately 50–60% of untreated CBS-deficient patients, ranging from borderline to moderate in severity.
Psychiatric symptoms including anxiety, obsessive-compulsive behaviour, schizophrenia-like episodes, and depression, which emerge in adolescence and adulthood.

Premature thromboembolism affecting arteries and veins — deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, myocardial infarction, or renal artery thrombosis — the leading cause of morbidity and early death.
Osteoporosis and pathological fractures from impaired collagen cross-linking, leading to spinal compression fractures in young adults.
Fair skin and hair (hypopigmentation) because excess homocysteine inhibits tyrosinase, reducing melanin production.
Malar flush — a characteristic pink blush on the cheeks — from dermal capillary dilatation.
Seizures, occurring in roughly 20% of untreated CBS-deficient patients, often preceding other neurological manifestations.
Livedo reticularis — a mottled, net-like purplish skin discolouration from microvascular dysfunction.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive nearsightedness (myopia) in a young child that worsens faster than expected for simple refractive error.
A slightly downward shift of the pupil relative to the iris in photographs, hinting at early lens subluxation.
Unexpectedly tall stature with very long fingers noted at a routine developmental assessment.
Elevated total plasma homocysteine on a newborn screening card or on a blood panel done for another reason.
emergencySigns: Sudden vision loss or a large new floater suggesting retinal detachment from complete lens dislocation.
Acute stroke symptoms — sudden facial droop, arm weakness, speech difficulty, or confusion — in a child or young adult.
Signs of deep vein thrombosis (leg swelling, calf pain) or pulmonary embolism (pleuritic chest pain, breathlessness, haemoptysis).
Acute neurological deterioration or seizure cluster not explained by intercurrent illness.

causes.description: Biallelic mutations in the CBS gene on chromosome 21q22.3 eliminate or severely reduce the enzyme that converts homocysteine and serine to cystathionine. Homocysteine and methionine both accumulate; cystathionine and cysteine are deficient. This is the cause in over 90% of classical homocystinuria cases.

Severe MTHFR mutations reduce production of 5-methyltetrahydrofolate, the methyl donor required by methionine synthase to convert homocysteine back to methionine. Plasma homocysteine rises; methionine is low or normal. This form presents earlier and more severely than CBS deficiency.

Mutations affecting cobalamin transport proteins (cblC through cblJ) impair methionine synthase activity, simultaneously causing methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria. These combined forms respond to hydroxocobalamin injections and are diagnosed by newborn screening or organic acid analysis.

Deficiency of hepatic methionine adenosyltransferase causes hypermethioninemia rather than frank homocystinuria in most cases, but in severe forms homocysteine rises. Demyelination rather than thromboembolism is the dominant complication.

Severe dietary deficiency of vitamin B6, folate, or vitamin B12 in non-genetic cases raises plasma homocysteine to mildly elevated levels; these cases are not classical homocystinuria but share some vascular risk. Correcting the deficiency normalises homocysteine.

riskFactors.description: Two defective CBS alleles are required to cause disease; each child of two carrier parents has a 25% chance of being affected. Over 160 different CBS mutations have been described, with missense mutations predominating.
Patients who lack cofactor responsiveness have higher plasma homocysteine levels with standard treatment and carry a greater vascular and thrombotic risk than pyridoxine-responsive patients.
A methionine-restricted diet is unpalatable and difficult to maintain, particularly in adolescence; dietary lapses raise homocysteine acutely and cumulatively increase vascular risk.

The risk of thromboembolic events rises steeply with plasma total homocysteine; reducing it below 50 μmol/L is associated with a substantially lower thrombosis incidence, though normal levels (<15 μmol/L) are ideal.
Surgery, prolonged bed rest, and dehydration greatly amplify thrombotic risk in homocystinuria patients; thromboprophylaxis is mandatory perioperatively.
Pregnancy increases coagulation tendency and the metabolic demand for folate and B12, raising homocysteine and thrombotic risk; specialist metabolic obstetric care is required throughout.
Both cofactors support homocysteine remethylation; deficiency on any background impairs treatment efficacy and raises homocysteine further.

affectedDemographics: Newborns and infants detected through newborn screening programmes
Children presenting in early school age with myopia, skeletal features, or intellectual difficulties
Young adults with unexplained stroke, DVT, or pulmonary embolism
Populations of Irish, Qatari, or other descent with higher carrier frequencies
Equal sex distribution; autosomal recessive inheritance
diagnosisOverview: Homocystinuria is most commonly detected through newborn screening by measuring total homocysteine or methionine on a dried blood spot; elevated methionine is the primary marker for CBS deficiency. In patients not screened at birth, the diagnosis is prompted by lens dislocation, Marfanoid features, stroke in a young person, or intellectual disability. Plasma amino acid analysis shows elevated methionine and homocysteine and low or absent cystathionine in CBS deficiency; remethylation defects show low methionine with elevated homocysteine. Total plasma homocysteine is measured by automated immunoassay or high-performance liquid chromatography; values above 100 μmol/L are strongly suggestive of CBS deficiency in the appropriate clinical context. Enzyme activity measurement in cultured fibroblasts or liver tissue, and CBS gene sequencing, confirm the diagnosis and identify pyridoxine responsiveness. A pyridoxine loading trial (100–500 mg/day for four to six weeks) distinguishes responsive from non-responsive patients. Ophthalmological slit-lamp examination reveals lens subluxation. Echocardiography may be needed to exclude aortic root dilatation in the Marfan syndrome differential, in which plasma homocysteine is normal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects CBS deficiency through elevated dried blood spot methionine; the standard first-line test in countries with expanded newborn screening.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small heel-prick blood sample taken at 24–72 hours of age; results typically returned within 1–2 weeks. A positive screen triggers confirmatory plasma amino acid measurement.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma amino acid analysis (methionine, homocysteine, cystathionine)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the amino acid disturbances characteristic of CBS deficiency: high methionine, high free and total homocysteine, and low or absent cystathionine.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A venous blood sample is sent to a specialist metabolic laboratory; results take 3–7 days. Samples must be processed quickly to prevent artefactual homocysteine changes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures all forms of circulating homocysteine; levels above 100 μmol/L are diagnostic of severe homocystinuria in the clinical context of CBS deficiency.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fasting blood sample is preferred; samples must be kept cold and processed within 30 minutes to avoid falsely high readings from red cell haemolysis.

diagnosticTests.test: CBS enzyme activity assay (cultured fibroblasts or liver biopsy)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly measures cystathionine beta-synthase activity; residual activity correlates with pyridoxine responsiveness and disease severity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Skin biopsy for fibroblast culture is the usual approach; fibroblasts are grown over several weeks before enzyme activity can be measured. Liver biopsy provides more direct data but is rarely performed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific mutations to confirm diagnosis, determine pyridoxine responsiveness by genotype-phenotype correlation, and enable carrier testing and prenatal diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is sent for next-generation sequencing of the CBS gene; results take 2–6 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects ectopia lentis (lens subluxation), the hallmark ocular finding; differentiates homocystinuria from Marfan syndrome, in which lenses dislocate upward rather than downward.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless examination by an ophthalmologist using a magnifying lamp; pupil dilation drops are usually applied first. Takes about 20 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of CBS deficiency aims to reduce plasma total homocysteine to as low as possible — ideally below 100 μmol/L and preferably below 50 μmol/L — to prevent vascular events and other complications. The first step is a pyridoxine (vitamin B6) responsiveness trial at 100–500 mg/day for 4–6 weeks; responders can often maintain adequate homocysteine control on pyridoxine alone together with folate and vitamin B12 supplementation, which are required cofactors for the remethylation pathway. Non-responders require a low-methionine diet: natural protein intake is restricted and supplemented with a methionine-free, cystine-enriched amino acid formula to meet protein requirements while limiting the substrate for homocysteine production. Betaine (trimethylglycine) is added to both pyridoxine-responsive and non-responsive patients who do not achieve adequate biochemical control; betaine donates a methyl group to remethylate homocysteine to methionine via betaine-homocysteine methyltransferase, bypassing the CBS block. Anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy is used in patients who have had a thrombotic event, and perioperative low-molecular-weight heparin is standard practice for all patients undergoing surgery or prolonged immobilisation. Ophthalmological follow-up monitors for lens subluxation progression; surgical lens extraction is considered when vision is threatened. Pyridoxine plus folate supplementation during pregnancy reduces the elevated thrombotic risk of gestation. In remethylation defects, hydroxocobalamin injections are the primary treatment. Lifelong monitoring of plasma homocysteine, amino acid levels, renal function, bone density, and ophthalmological status is required.

medicalTreatments.name: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose pyridoxine at 100–500 mg/day activates residual CBS enzyme activity in pyridoxine-responsive patients, normalising or substantially reducing plasma homocysteine. Always co-administered with folate and B12 to support cofactor-dependent remethylation pathways.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 50% of CBS-deficient patients are pyridoxine-responsive; in these, homocysteine can be normalised in 30–50% and maintained below 50 μmol/L in the majority with continued therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Betaine (trimethylglycine)
medicalTreatments.description: Betaine donates a methyl group via betaine-homocysteine methyltransferase to convert homocysteine to methionine, bypassing the CBS defect. Oral doses of 3–9 g/day in divided doses are used in both pyridoxine-responsive and non-responsive patients who require additional homocysteine lowering.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical trials show betaine reduces total plasma homocysteine by approximately 30–60% in non-responsive patients and prevents or delays vascular events; effectiveness exceeds dietary restriction alone in most reported cohorts.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-methionine diet with amino acid formula
medicalTreatments.description: Natural protein (mainly animal and legume protein) is restricted to reduce methionine intake, the substrate for homocysteine production. A methionine-free, cystine-enriched amino acid formula provides essential amino acids and calories. Dietitian input is essential for lifelong management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dietary management in non-responsive patients reduces homocysteine by 40–70% when adhered to; studies show lower thromboembolism rates and improved cognitive outcomes in patients maintained below 100 μmol/L compared with uncontrolled cohorts.

medicalTreatments.name: Folic acid and vitamin B12 supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Both cofactors support the remethylation of homocysteine to methionine; deficiency of either worsens homocysteine accumulation in any form of homocystinuria. Supplementation is universal in all patients regardless of type.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalisation of folate and B12 status reduces homocysteine levels by 10–25% in deficient individuals and optimises the efficacy of other treatments.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxocobalamin (for cobalamin remethylation defects)
medicalTreatments.description: Parenteral hydroxocobalamin injections are the primary treatment for cblC, cblE, cblG, and related cobalamin metabolism defects, restoring methionine synthase activity and normalising homocysteine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In cblC deficiency, early hydroxocobalamin treatment normalises homocysteine in approximately 80% of patients and significantly improves neurodevelopmental outcomes compared with untreated or late-treated cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Anticoagulation (after thrombotic events)
medicalTreatments.description: Long-term anticoagulation with warfarin or a direct oral anticoagulant is recommended after any thrombotic event; perioperative and antenatal low-molecular-weight heparin is given prophylactically to all patients undergoing high-risk procedures.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: While randomised data are lacking due to disease rarity, retrospective series show at least 50–70% reduction in recurrent thrombotic events in anticoagulated patients compared with historical controls.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical lens extraction
surgicalOptions.description: Performed when ectopia lentis causes severe visual impairment, uncontrolled secondary glaucoma, or imminent retinal detachment risk. Requires specialist anaesthetic protocols to minimise perioperative thrombotic risk, including pre-operative intensification of homocysteine-lowering therapy and low-molecular-weight heparin cover.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual outcomes are good when performed before retinal detachment occurs; reported visual acuity improvement in approximately 70–80% of operated eyes in specialist series.

alternativeTreatments.name: L-cysteine supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Because CBS deficiency impairs cystathionine and cysteine production, supplementary L-cysteine is sometimes added to the amino acid formula to support glutathione synthesis and meet metabolic demands.

alternativeTreatments.name: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) supplementation in MTHFR defects
alternativeTreatments.description: Severe MTHFR deficiency is stabilised by riboflavin, which is a required cofactor; supplementation alongside folate and betaine forms the standard regimen for this sub-type.

whySeeSpecialist: Homocystinuria management requires a metabolic disease specialist because achieving biochemical targets demands combination therapy that must be individually titrated based on homocysteine monitoring, amino acid levels, and pyridoxine responsiveness. Thrombotic risk management, dietary prescription, ophthalmological surveillance, bone density monitoring, and neuropsychiatric support all require coordination across multiple disciplines that a metabolic centre can provide under one umbrella.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a metabolic medicine specialist or inherited metabolic disease physician at a centre that has a paediatric-to-adult transition programme, given that homocystinuria is a lifelong condition. The centre should offer in-house or partnered plasma homocysteine monitoring with rapid turnaround, specialist metabolic dietitian input, and ophthalmological and vascular surgery links. Ask whether the programme has experience with perioperative homocystinuria management and with the psychiatric and neurological complications of the condition in adults.
preventionStrategies: Enrolment in newborn screening is the most effective prevention strategy, allowing treatment before symptoms develop and almost eliminating the risk of intellectual disability and early thrombosis.
Genetic carrier screening for couples with a family history of homocystinuria; prenatal diagnosis by CBS gene analysis or enzyme assay is available.
Strict lifelong adherence to pyridoxine, betaine, and/or dietary restriction to maintain total plasma homocysteine below target.
Perioperative thromboprophylaxis with low-molecular-weight heparin and hydration for all surgical procedures.
Specialist management throughout pregnancy to minimise thrombotic risk.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere consistently to the prescribed low-methionine diet and amino acid formula supplementation; even short-term dietary lapses raise homocysteine meaningfully.
Maintain adequate hydration, particularly during travel, illness, and hot weather, to reduce blood viscosity and thrombotic risk.
Avoid prolonged immobility on long flights or during convalescence; use compression stockings and anticoagulant prophylaxis as directed.
Wear medical ID jewellery indicating homocystinuria and thrombotic risk to alert emergency personnel.
Pursue regular ophthalmological review; early lens subluxation may be managed conservatively with spectacle correction before surgery is needed.
Engage with a metabolic psychologist or psychiatrist if anxiety, OCD, or mood symptoms emerge, as these are part of the condition's natural history.

recommended: Methionine-free, cystine-enriched amino acid formula to provide adequate protein and essential nutrients within methionine restriction
Low-methionine natural foods: most fruits, vegetables, and some low-protein staples (low-protein bread, pasta)
Folate-rich vegetables (spinach, asparagus, broccoli) to support remethylation
Adequate vitamin B12 from fortified foods or supplements to optimise homocysteine remethylation
avoid: High-methionine foods: all meat, poultry, fish, eggs, dairy products, legumes, and nuts in amounts that exceed the prescribed methionine allowance
Methionine supplements or high-protein commercial supplements not designed for metabolic disorders
Alcohol in excess, which impairs folate and B12 metabolism and raises homocysteine

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise is encouraged and reduces cardiovascular risk, which is already elevated in homocystinuria. Contact sports with a high risk of falls should be approached cautiously given osteoporosis and the risk of ocular injury to subluxated lenses. High-impact activities are best discussed with the metabolic team and orthopaedic specialist given bone fragility. Patients should stay well hydrated during and after exercise to prevent haemoconcentration and the thrombotic risk it confers.
dailyManagement: Take pyridoxine, betaine, and vitamin supplements at prescribed doses and times each day.
Follow the methionine-restricted diet using a portion guide provided by the metabolic dietitian.
Schedule plasma total homocysteine monitoring at least every 3–6 months and attend dose reviews promptly when levels are outside target.
Carry emergency thromboprophylaxis information for hospital or surgical admissions.
Attend annual ophthalmology review to track lens position and visual acuity.
Report any new visual disturbance, limb pain or swelling, or neurological symptoms immediately to the metabolic team or emergency services.
prognosis: Prognosis depends critically on early detection and treatment. Patients identified through newborn screening and treated from birth have near-normal intellectual development; mean IQ in treated patients has been reported around 95–105 compared with 57–79 in untreated historical cohorts. Pyridoxine-responsive patients treated from birth have a very low thromboembolism rate and near-normal life expectancy. Non-responsive patients treated with dietary restriction and betaine still face residual vascular risk proportional to their degree of homocysteine control; cumulative thromboembolism rates fall from over 50% by age 30 in untreated patients to roughly 10–20% in well-controlled patients. Lens dislocation is preventable or delayed with good homocysteine control. Late-onset psychiatric complications and progressive skeletal disease remain challenges even with good metabolic management. Overall, with modern multidisciplinary treatment, most patients with homocystinuria survive well into adulthood with meaningful quality of life.
recoveryTimeline: Within weeks to months of starting pyridoxine, betaine, and dietary therapy, plasma homocysteine falls toward target and the thrombotic risk reduction begins. Existing lens subluxation stabilises with good homocysteine control but does not reverse. Intellectual development in newborn-screened patients follows a normal trajectory from birth; no cognitive catch-up is possible for damage that occurred before treatment began in late-diagnosed patients. Bone density may improve modestly over years with good metabolic control and calcium and vitamin D supplementation.
complications: Thromboembolism — deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, and myocardial infarction — the leading cause of death in untreated and poorly controlled patients.
Lens dislocation (ectopia lentis) causing blindness, retinal detachment, and secondary glaucoma if progressive and untreated.
Intellectual disability and psychiatric disorders including anxiety, OCD, and psychosis in unscreened or late-treated patients.
Osteoporosis with spinal compression fractures and long bone fractures from impaired collagen cross-linking.
Progressive myopia and retinal degeneration from chronic homocysteine toxicity to ocular structures.

supportResources.name: HCU Network America
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organisation for homocystinuria providing education, peer support, research news, and connections to specialist metabolic centres in the US.

supportResources.name: British Inherited Metabolic Disease Group (BIMDG)
supportResources.description: UK clinical group providing guidelines and resources for management of homocystinuria and other inherited metabolic diseases.

supportResources.name: NORD — Homocystinuria Overview
supportResources.description: Plain-language disease overview from the National Organization for Rare Disorders covering causes, symptoms, and treatment.

sources.title: Mudd SH et al. The natural history of homocystinuria due to cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency. American Journal of Human Genetics, 1985

confusedWithConditions.name: Marfan syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Marfan syndrome shares tall stature, arachnodactyly, and lens dislocation (upward vs. downward in homocystinuria) but plasma homocysteine is normal and it is caused by FBN1 fibrillin mutations rather than CBS deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Methylmalonic acidemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cobalamin-type combined homocystinuria and methylmalonic acidemia may present similarly to remethylation-type homocystinuria; elevated methylmalonate on urine organic acids distinguishes it and directs treatment to hydroxocobalamin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Phenylketonuria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PKU also causes intellectual disability, fair skin and hair, and is managed with dietary restriction; it is distinguished by elevated plasma phenylalanine rather than homocysteine and does not carry a thrombotic risk.

coOccurringConditions.name: Other urea cycle and amino acid disorders
Urea cycle disorders (co-management in metabolic clinics)

relatedConditions.name: Phenylketonuria
relatedConditions.relevance: Another autosomal recessive amino acid disorder causing intellectual disability and managed with lifelong dietary protein restriction and specialist metabolic follow-up.

relatedConditions.name: Methylmalonic acidemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria from cobalamin defects requires simultaneous management of both metabolic abnormalities with hydroxocobalamin.

relatedConditions.name: Cystinosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another inherited disorder of amino acid/cystine metabolism causing progressive multi-organ disease; contrasted with homocystinuria to help differentiate these rare conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Marfan syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common phenotypic mimic of homocystinuria, sharing lens dislocation and skeletal features; plasma homocysteine measurement is a key step in differentiating the two conditions.

faqs.question: What is homocystinuria?
faqs.answer: Homocystinuria is an inherited metabolic disorder in which the amino acid homocysteine accumulates in the blood and urine due to enzyme defects in its processing, most commonly deficiency of cystathionine beta-synthase. It causes vascular damage, eye lens dislocation, skeletal changes resembling Marfan syndrome, and intellectual disability if untreated.

faqs.question: How is homocystinuria inherited?
faqs.answer: Classical homocystinuria is autosomal recessive: both copies of the CBS gene must be mutant for the disease to occur. Both parents are healthy carriers. Each child of two carriers has a 25% chance of being affected and a 50% chance of being a carrier.

faqs.question: What is the main danger in homocystinuria?
faqs.answer: The leading danger is premature thromboembolism — blood clots in veins and arteries — which can cause stroke, pulmonary embolism, or heart attack in children and young adults. Without treatment, cumulative thrombosis risk exceeds 50% by age 30.

faqs.question: How is homocystinuria treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the type. Most patients with CBS deficiency are tried on high-dose pyridoxine (vitamin B6) first; about half respond with normalised homocysteine. Non-responders use a low-methionine diet, betaine, and folic acid. All patients take folate and vitamin B12 to support remethylation pathways.

faqs.question: Is homocystinuria the same as high homocysteine?
faqs.answer: No. High homocysteine from dietary deficiency of folate or B12 is common and only mildly elevated. Homocystinuria refers to severe hereditary elevation from enzyme defects, where values can exceed 100–200 μmol/L (normal is below 15 μmol/L) and carries a very high vascular risk.

faqs.question: What eye problems does homocystinuria cause?
faqs.answer: The hallmark ocular finding is ectopia lentis — subluxation of the eye lens, typically downward. This causes severe myopia, secondary glaucoma, and risk of retinal detachment. Early metabolic treatment can stabilise lens position; surgical lens removal is needed when vision is severely threatened.

faqs.question: Does homocystinuria cause intellectual disability?
faqs.answer: Untreated CBS deficiency causes intellectual disability in roughly 50–60% of patients. When detected at birth through newborn screening and treated promptly, nearly all children develop normal intelligence. The damage is largely preventable with early treatment.

faqs.question: What is the difference between homocystinuria and Marfan syndrome?
faqs.answer: Both cause tall stature, long limbs, and lens dislocation, but in homocystinuria the lens dislocates downward-inward and plasma homocysteine is very high. In Marfan syndrome, the lens dislocates upward, homocysteine is normal, and the FBN1 fibrillin gene is mutated rather than CBS.

faqs.question: Is surgery safe for someone with homocystinuria?
faqs.answer: Surgery is high-risk in homocystinuria because it greatly increases thrombotic risk. Specialists intensify homocysteine-lowering therapy before the procedure and cover the perioperative period with low-molecular-weight heparin. Anaesthetic teams need to be informed so precautions are taken.

faqs.question: What does betaine do in homocystinuria?
faqs.answer: Betaine (trimethylglycine) donates a methyl group to convert homocysteine to methionine via a different enzyme pathway, bypassing the CBS deficiency. It reduces plasma homocysteine by 30–60% and is used in combination with diet and pyridoxine in most patients.

faqs.question: Can homocystinuria be detected in newborns?
faqs.answer: Yes. Elevated methionine on the newborn dried blood spot screen is the primary marker for CBS deficiency. Early detection allows treatment before intellectual damage or vascular events occur, which is why inclusion in expanded newborn screening programmes is recommended.

faqs.question: How does homocystinuria affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Pregnancy increases coagulation and folate demand, raising thrombotic risk. Specialist metabolic obstetric care is required throughout, including intensified homocysteine monitoring and anticoagulation. Most women with well-controlled homocystinuria can have successful pregnancies with appropriate planning.

faqs.question: What causes osteoporosis in homocystinuria?
faqs.answer: Homocysteine interferes with the cross-linking of collagen in bone matrix, weakening bone structure. Chronic low cystathionine and cysteine levels may also impair connective tissue repair. This leads to osteoporosis and spinal compression fractures in young adults, sometimes even in teenagers.

faqs.question: What foods are restricted in homocystinuria?
faqs.answer: All high-protein foods naturally rich in methionine are restricted: meat, poultry, fish, eggs, dairy, legumes, and most nuts. Patients use a methionine-free amino acid formula to meet protein needs. Fruits, most vegetables, and specially manufactured low-protein foods are allowed.

faqs.question: How often is plasma homocysteine checked?
faqs.answer: For stable patients on established therapy, plasma total homocysteine and amino acids are checked every 3–6 months. More frequent monitoring is needed after dose adjustments, during illness, before surgery, or during pregnancy.

faqs.question: Can homocystinuria be cured?
faqs.answer: Current treatments control the condition but do not correct the underlying gene defect. Gene therapy approaches are in early preclinical and clinical research. CBS enzyme replacement therapy is also being investigated. For now, lifelong daily treatment is required.

faqs.question: What psychiatric problems occur in homocystinuria?
faqs.answer: Adults with homocystinuria have elevated rates of anxiety disorders, obsessive-compulsive disorder, depression, and in some cases psychosis or schizophrenia-like episodes. These are part of the disorder's natural history and benefit from psychological support and, when needed, appropriate pharmacotherapy.

faqs.question: Are siblings at risk if one child has homocystinuria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Siblings of an affected child each have a 25% chance of also being affected (if both parents are carriers) and a 50% chance of being a carrier. Biochemical testing of all siblings at diagnosis is recommended so that affected siblings can be treated immediately.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy for someone with homocystinuria?
faqs.answer: Pyridoxine-responsive patients treated from birth have near-normal life expectancy. Non-responsive patients with well-controlled homocysteine below 50 μmol/L also have substantially improved outcomes. Untreated, thrombosis-related mortality reduces life expectancy considerably, with some patients dying in their twenties.

faqs.question: What is a pyridoxine responsiveness trial?
faqs.answer: A pyridoxine trial gives 100–500 mg of vitamin B6 daily for 4–6 weeks while monitoring plasma homocysteine. A reduction of homocysteine to normal or near-normal range defines a responsive patient. Responsiveness is the most important predictor of treatment ease and long-term prognosis.

metaDescription: Homocystinuria is an inherited amino acid disorder causing blood clots, lens dislocation, and bone problems. Learn symptoms, treatment, and dietary management.
